fuzzy_prd: We need a batching primitive for our in-memory key-value store client that lets developers group many commands together and dispatch them in a single round-trip instead of paying network latency once per command. Commands issued inside the batch are collected and sent together; the batch itself returns the ordered list of replies, and each individual command issued inside the batch also gives back a deferred handle whose value becomes available once the batch executes. Batches can be nested inside each other and can contain database-switch commands that take effect immediately for the rest of that batch and persist afterward. When a store command fails, the batch should surface the failure as a normalized error carrying the store's own message — not a raw Ruby exception. Handles for commands that were queued after a failed command should be clearly unresolved rather than silently returning stale values. Reply types should be preserved: a field/value reply comes back as a map, a multi-element reply as a list — handle that the same way non-batched typed replies are handled, just inside the pipeline context.

trigger_keywords: what are the type-tagged scalar formats
how are different reply types rendered
what prefix does an integer reply have
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Type-tagged scalars: nil reply -> 'nil'; integer -> 'int:<n>'; boolean true -> 'bool:true'; boolean false -> 'bool:false'; string -> 'str:<text>'; float -> 'float:<n>'. Maps are rendered as 'map:<size>
' followed by '<key>=<type-tagged-value>
' per entry sorted by key. Lists are rendered as 'list:<size>
' followed by '<index>=<type-tagged-value>
' per element.

trigger_keywords: what error is produced when a handle is read before the batch executes
what happens when you read a future before the pipeline runs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Reading a handle before the batch executes raises Redis::FutureNotReady. The adapter catches this and emits 'error:value_not_ready' (note colon separator, not equals). After a failed batch, unresolved handles also yield 'h<index>:value_not_ready'.

trigger_keywords: what error lines are produced when a batch command fails
how is a store command rejection reported
what is the batch_error output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a batch fails due to a rejected command (Redis::CommandError), the output is: 'error:command_error\ndetail:<store error message>\n'. The detail line carries the store's own error text verbatim.

trigger_keywords: how does a batch return its replies
what is the format of the replies action output
how is the reply count reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
The 'replies' action prints: 'count:<n>
' then one line per reply as '<index>:<type-tagged-scalar>
'. An empty batch prints 'count:0
'. Each queued command contributes exactly one reply entry; a command with no value contributes 'nil'.

trigger_keywords: does a database switch in a batch persist after the batch
what happens to the connection's active database after a batched select
does select inside pipelined affect later commands
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A SELECT command issued inside a batch takes effect for the rest of that batch and persists after the batch returns. The connection reports the switched-to database as the current database. In a nested batch, the most deeply selected database becomes current.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle that the same way non-batched typed replies are handled, just inside the pipeline context
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Redis client's reply coercion (hgetall returns a Hash, keys returns an Array) applies inside pipelined blocks. The dispatcher calls render_structured() which formats Hash replies as map:<size> + key=value lines (sorted by key) and Array replies as list:<size> + index=value lines.

oracle_data.fallback_response: Please refer to the start.md specification. Actions: apply, replies, handles, handle_early, batch_error, handles_unresolved, typed, select_persist, current_db.

feature1_batched_writes.json.description: Queue several write commands inside a single batch, then read the resulting store state with ordinary follow-up commands. The batch may mix single-key writes and multi-key writes and may contain a nested batch; every queued command must be applied to the store, preserving the order in which it was queued. Each follow-up read prints its reply as a type-tagged scalar so both the value and its reply type are observable.

feature2_batch_reply_array.json.description: Run a batch and inspect the value the batch itself returns: an ordered list containing one reply per queued command, in queue order. The output first prints the number of replies, then each reply by index as a type-tagged scalar. An empty batch returns an empty list.

feature3_1_handle_values.json.description: A command issued inside a batch returns a deferred handle rather than an immediate value. After the batch executes, each handle resolves to the reply of its command. The batch here adds the same member to a set twice; the first add reports success (member created) and the second reports no change (member already present), demonstrating that handles carry per-command boolean replies. Nested batches contribute handles too.

feature3_2_handle_not_ready_early.json.description: Attempt to read a deferred handle's value while the batch is still open (before it has been sent and executed). The value is not yet available, so the read is rejected with a normalized not-ready error instead of returning a bogus value.

feature4_1_batch_error.json.description: When a batch contains a command the store rejects, executing the batch fails and the failure is surfaced as a normalized command error carrying the store's own error text. This covers both an unrecognized command and a recognized command given an argument of the wrong kind.

cases.expected_output:
error:command_error
detail:ERR unknown command 'doesnt_exist', with args beginning with:

error:command_error
detail:ERR value is not an integer or out of range

feature4_2_handles_unresolved.json.description: A batch queues a command the store rejects, followed by further commands whose deferred handles are tracked. Because the batch fails on the rejected command, the tracked handles are never resolved; reading each one afterward yields the normalized not-ready status rather than a value.

feature5_1_field_map_reply.json.description: Reply-type coercion applies inside a batch: a command that returns a field/value collection is decoded into a map, not a flat list. The store is first populated with one field, then the field-map command is run inside a batch; its reply is rendered as a map of key to type-tagged value.

feature5_2_list_reply.json.description: Reply-type coercion applies inside a batch: a command that returns multiple elements is decoded into a list. One key is stored, then a key-listing command is run inside a batch; its reply is rendered as a list with a count followed by each element as a type-tagged scalar.

feature6_1_select_persist.json.description: A database-switch command issued inside a batch takes effect for the rest of that batch and persists after the batch returns. One database is written before the batch; the batch switches to a second database and writes there; probing each database afterward shows each value landed in its own database.

feature6_2_current_db.json.description: After a batch that switches the active database, the connection reports the switched-to database as its current database. This holds for a single switch and for a switch performed inside a nested batch, where the most deeply selected database becomes current.
